speaker: interviewer
text: --mugshot. We're only focusing in on the paper. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] Today is September 4, 1996. The survivor is Lili Meier, M-E-I-E-R, maiden name Jacob. The interviewer is Sue Rosenblum, R-O-S-E-N-B-L-U-M. We are in Longwood, Florida, the United States of America. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: All set.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now I have to do my schtick next to her? Is that all right, [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, it's fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now I have to repeat this, sitting next to you. [INAUDIBLE] next to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Then-- no, no, no, because we have to be in the camera at the same time. Today is September 4, 1996. We are conducting an interview with Lili Meier, M-E-I-E-R, maiden name Jacob, J-A-C-O-B. I'm Sue Rosenblum, R-O-S-E-N-B-L-U-M. We are in Longwood, Florida, the United States of America. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. [INAUDIBLE] for you to come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are we paused? I'm going back. Would you tell us your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Lili, maiden name Jacob, last name Meier, M-E-I-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Bilky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in the Carpathian Mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country is that in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It used to be Czechoslovakia after World War I. And I had a-- we had a very good childhood. My father was the provider. And everything was going well.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on January 16, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today I'm 70. I was-- and January is my birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Martin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a businessman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: A sister, I never had. But I had five wonderful brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ari, Zisl, Hershey, Zrilu, and Selig.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your home like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a happy home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe Shabbat for me, what Shabbat was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbat was like-- Thursday night, my mother started with the baking. And then the [? challah ?] and the Danish went in on Friday in the oven. It was a very happy childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she make special foods for Shabbat or Pesach--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Shabbat was the usual chicken and chicken soup and matzo ball and-- like the baking, she did all herself. It was just a joyous home and joyous parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have family that lived nearby, grandparents or cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they lived in the same town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would they come and spend holidays with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We mostly went to them, because they all wanted to have us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other languages were spoken in your home? Well, what languages were spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bilky.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of us, we were happy there. This is where our parents, our grandparents, used to live and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism in school or while you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, on the contrary. The majority of my friends weren't Jewish. But we got along, the Jews and the non-Jews. We got along very well. We had wonderful neighbors, and we would do for one another. And it was always reciprocated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish community like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a nice Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about 5,000 people lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy any special interests or hobbies when you were younger, things that you liked to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was more of a thinker and a doer. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, it's not [? think. ?] You know, everything was just wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think about your future when you were younger, things that you wanted to do with your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had friends whose-- whose family moved to America. And then they would come and visit their-- their parents. And that's one thing. When I saw those people, you know, are coming to-- to Bilky, and the way they used to be dressed, and I said to myself, one day, I want to go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any clubs when you were young, organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a club on Saturday. They would gather. But I was always just the bystander, because I was everywhere-- weddings, Jewish weddings, Gentile. I was always a very curious child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any special books or literature that was found in your home when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother was a reader, just like my Eric is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any special things that you liked to read?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, usually my mother would borrow a book from the library. And then she would say, sweetheart, I think you would like this book. And it was about Tolstoy and those history books.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your brothers bar mitzvah'd?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Not the two youngest were, because.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what their bar mitzvahs were like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were going on a Saturday to the synagogue. And then family from out of town were arrive. And it was a very festive time for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your life change when the war began?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] on the last [INAUDIBLE] Passover, '44, somehow it was known in the town-- neighboring towns that they going to take the Jews and resettle them in Poland. And it was like a-- a-- you know, story. But soon it changed. I decided in 1943, because they took up a-- my father's permit. And I decided that, because a lot of youngsters were-- went to Budapest to work-- and that's what I went, too. And I would send the money home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Up until 1943, when you left to go to Budapest, did you receive any word of what was happening in Germany and Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard about Poland. But from Germany, it was later on, a while later that we found out that what's going to go on. We had a radio in the house which were-- we were not allowed to. And we heard how he was [INAUDIBLE]. And they were talking about Hitler, and carrying on, and saying that he recruits the rich Jews, and the wives have to come and scrub the-- the [INAUDIBLE]. How-- how would you say that where the soldiers stayed? So the-- those [? stop, ?] you know, German Jewish people were right away known as what they gonna-- you know, what's going to happen to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your father's permit was taken away, how did he support the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very rough, very rough. But when he heard after dinner on that Passover night in '43, first, he says, no. I have six-- I have six children. And if I can feed five, I can feed a sixth one. No child of mine will go to Budapest. And then after dinner, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we sat down, and we talked. [SIGHS] And he says, yeah, I know you are very strong-willed child. But we let you do, because we trust you. You never were a mischief. You always know what you were doing. And he's the one who gave in. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I went to Budapest. I mean, we got on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friends from across the street of ours. They were friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther Bohm. She's in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you took with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have much to take with me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So I got on the train. And that train would stop in every other town or city. And there--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like-- excuse me-- when you left your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was rough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened was-- can I go back to the train?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was after Passover. And the train was full of people who were going to Budapest. And there was a lady sitting. She must have been in her early 50s. And she would-- she asked me do I speak Hungarian? And I said, yes. And where are you go? Where are you going? I says, I'm going to Budapest. I want to help out my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, well, I-- and she introduced herself. She says, I'm in charge of a big orphanage. Would you like to come and work for us? I says, no. I says, these days are very bitter. It's a very bitter pill to swallow. And I says, and they'll put me in a more depressed mood. And I said, I-- I was always taught by my parents that you should always feed an orphan the best way you can. And she says, well, we are quite a while back from Budapest. Why don't you think about it? And-- and I says, well, I-- I decided not to really go. She says, well, as we are [? progressing ?] on the train, we can talk. She says, well, you can-- you can come and work for us. And I was all of 18. And all you have to do is make the list for the grocery, and you'll go to the store. And you'll order everything, and they'll deliver it. And I says, well, let me think about it. But instead to going to the orphanage, I went to the Jewish community, because they were finding jobs for us. And when I came there-- and there was a man, he says, what can we do for you? And I told him I would like to get a permit and work. Oh. He-- he had a list there in front of him. He says, oh, you don't have to worry. You have a job. And I says, where do I have a job? He says-- and he told me the name of the orphanage, because the lady told me to. And he says, we cannot give you, because she wants you. And you'll-- you'll fit in just fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went along. But as the days passed, and I missed my parents-- I was never away from them so far-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And by the time I came to the orphanage, I was knocked out, because I didn't sleep for days and nights. And I found a friend there that I didn't see for about four years. We were friends at-- at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Raisy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hungarian, yeah. R-O-N-A or R-O-N-O. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Street, 52nd Street. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got to the orphanage, and you met this friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we were very, you know, shocked to meet one another after so many years. And she sounded so glad that we are together. We grew up together, and now we're still friends. And I said I'm very happy to-- to have found you, too. At least I'll feel close that I have somebody that I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I liked my job. And I decided to stay there. There were two children. Oh-- Oh, let me go back. Can I? Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was actually not a real orphanage. That was when they start taking the Slovakian Jews and the-- and the Czech Jews. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] And the people would run away with the children, the parents. And this orphanage was known to take care only of people who left their houses, their homes. And there were active kids. We-- we-- there was a little-- there was a [INAUDIBLE] that orphanage. And the kids didn't go to school, because we had teachers there. And they really took care of those children.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was an incident once that I witnessed that a man came over, and he took one of the little girls on his lap. And he kissed this kid. She was about four years old. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the teacher came over. And she says, can-- can we take Martha away, because she has to go to sleep? She's very tired. And then a couple of days later, I noticed that this-- the teacher was drilling this kid. Martha, who was that man? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She started crying, and she shook her [? hands. ?] She says, don't talk to me. I'm very sad. Finally, they calmed her down, and she stopped crying. But she also had a brother there. There were two of them-- Robi, and her name was Martha. And there were about, I would say, about 100 kids, all for the same reason, that orphanage. And [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] she said, Martha, who-- who was that man? Tell me. I won't tell anybody. And she started crying and crying. All of the sudden, she said, that's my mother's husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- the teachers trained those children not to reveal, because we had to-- and they were coming, the officials, to check what kind of an orphanage. And we were like, say, about 500 feet from a munition factory there. And those sirens were terrible. The kids were scared. We also-- We also had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] downstairs-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It was, when the kids, you know, couldn't go out and play, so they would play down in that-- I forgot how you call. And those kids somehow, I'll never forget, and I never did forget. Once I saw a lady looking through the fence where the kids were playing on, you know, on a sunny day. And this lady somehow passed out. She was on the other side of the fence. And the kids were very sad, because she didn't come to look for them, look at them. And I just never forgot those kids. Can I skip a little bit? Well, I'd like to try to keep it in the sequence. So how long did you remain in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: 13 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during that time, did you have any contact with your parents and your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. A neighbor-- we didn't have a telephone there, but a neighbor told me that I could use the phone any time I want to call my parents. I was very lonely. I missed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you return to Bilky at all while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went a few times. And then one day, we heard what's coming up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we heard that they take away-- they took [INAUDIBLE]. They took so far away the-- the Jews from Slovakia, from the Czech countries. And our country-- where I was born, Bilky, became Hungary. And in order for us to be able to survive from Bilky, we needed to prove that, you know, we are Jews, and we do live in Hungary, which wasn't true. But that's-- that's how they, you know, let it go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did-- what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call those people that-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I was-- I went to order the-- in the grocery store, what we needed. And when I came back, a friend of mine came there, and she said, so you're gonna stay in Budapest? I said, no. Well, I'm gonna stay, because they're gonna take us away to Poland somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, aren't you coming home to your parents? She said, no. I want to stay here. That's OK. Well, on the way to the grocery store, I made a U-turn. And I-- [? when ?] I heard a rally there. And the rally was in German. And I didn't understand German at that time. So there were so many people, and he was screaming and banging there on the table in Germany. And one lady says in Hungarian, well, it looks like this man came for all the Jews, to take all the Jews away. And I just walked away. I didn't want to say nothing. What if they arrest me or something? I just walked away. And I ended up going to the train. I had enough money on me to go-- to go home. And my-- this friend of mine hollered after me, you're crazy. Why don't you come-- stay here? What if something happens? And I said, if something is gonna happen, I want to be there, too. Otherwise, I would never forgive myself. And I ended up going home. We were home for two weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one night, on a Thursday night-- it was after Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: '43. And [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] my mother and I decided to prepare the-- the baking for tomorrow morning and then the cooking. And all of a sudden, it was like 12:00, 1:00-- 1:00 in the morning, there was a knock on-- on-- on our door. We never had the doors locked. It wasn't needed to lock doors there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked my mother, should I open the door? She says, yes. You can open the door. And a neighbor came in. And he says he just came from a meeting that they're going to uproot us, and we-- we are going to Poland. Was this neighbor Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this neighbor Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isaac something. I don't remember his last name.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take a tape break now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why don't you go have your coffee?
